nativeRange.habitat: Found in groups along outer reef slopes at depths of approximately 4–30 m, typically 10–30 m.

diet.notes: An active planktivore; tends to accept frozen mysis shrimp and brine shrimp readily. Has been known to accept quality flake or pellet foods with conditioning. Frequent small feedings throughout the day are generally advised — two to three times daily — since this species has a high metabolic rate. A refugium seeded with copepods and amphipods is commonly considered beneficial as supplemental live food.

goodWith: other anthias species in large systems
avoidWith: aggressive species that may outcompete for food

summary: A vibrant orange-and-yellow anthias from the Western Indian Ocean, generally best kept in groups of one male per four to six females; tends to thrive in reef aquariums with frequent feedings and open swimming space.
careNotes: Carberryi Anthias are protogynous hermaphrodites — all individuals are born female, and the dominant individual in a group will transition to male if the male is lost. They are generally best kept in groups of five or more, with one male per four to six females or juveniles. Intraspecific aggression among males has been reported, so keeping more than one male in smaller systems is usually not advisable. These fish tend to be very active mid-water swimmers and have been noted to help draw out shier tankmates. A tightly fitted lid is commonly advised, as anthias have been known to jump. Sources differ on their native range: LiveAquaria lists the Maldives specifically, while FishBase documents the broader Western Indian Ocean, from South Africa northward and east to the Maldives.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been rarely documented for this species. In the wild, they form harems with one dominant male. If the male is removed or dies, the dominant female may change sex and assume the male role. No detailed captive breeding protocol is widely established.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (13 cm TL), temperature range (24.3–27.9°C), native range (Western Indian Ocean to Maldives and South Africa), and taxonomy. FishBase places this species in family Anthiadidae (elevated from Anthiadinae within Serranidae), superseding LiveAquaria's listing of Serranidae. Temperature averages were computed across both sources: LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), FishBase 24.3–27.9°C; averaged to min 23°C / max 27°C. Reef2Reef wiki returned HTTP 403; BlueZoo Aquatics returned no species-specific data; Fishkeeping World and TFH Magazine returned 404 for this species.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely achieved; haremic sex-change biology means a mated group (one male, several females) is needed. The dominant female can transition to male if the male is lost, but conditioning breeding pairs in captivity has been reported as difficult.
